{{Short description|Psychological phenomenon}}
[[File:red-dress1.jpg|thumb|Red dress effect|199x199px]]The '''red dress effect''', which can be broadened to the general '''red-attraction effect''', the '''red-romance effect''', or the '''romantic red effect''', is a [[phenomenon]]{{Clarify|reason=Is this theory well proven enough to call it a phenomenon? Or should we be calling it a hypothesis?|date=March 2023}} in which the color red increases physical attraction, sexual desire, and romantic sentiments in comparison to other colors.

It has been asserted that this effect acts [[subconscious]]ly because participants rarely report that they used color in their attractiveness judgments.<ref>{{cite web|last=Strain|first=Daniel|title=The Red-Dress Effect|url=https://www.science.org/content/article/red-dress-effect}}</ref> In past decades, increased interest in color psychology has prompted multiple studies to investigate the red-attraction effect and the extent of this association. The [[scientific literature]] on the red-attraction effect is mixed, with sound evidence that both support and oppose the validity of the connection.

==Hypothesis==
[[File:Proceedings_of_the_Zoological_Society_of_London_(9518378800).jpg|thumb|Red face [[uakari]] monkey.]]

===Evolution===
A plant's [[Flower#Attraction methods|entomophilous flowers]] make a display when fertile to attract pollinating insects, bats, birds or other animals. In the wild, when many species of non-human [[primate]] females become [[fertility|fertile]], their [[estrogen]] level rises, which causes their [[blood vessel]]s to open up, leading to [[Flushing (physiology)|redness on the skin]], especially near the face,<ref>[http://rsos.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/2/7/150145 "Proximate causes of the red face of the bald uakari monkey" Authors P. Mayor, J. Mamani, D. Montes, C. González-Crespo, M. A. Sebastián, M. Bowler. Published 29 July 2015.DOI: 10.1098/rsos.150145]</ref> chest and [[genitalia]].<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Wickings|first1=J. M.|year=2004|title=Sexual swelling in mandrills (Mandrillus sphinx): A test of the reliable indicator hypothesis|journal=Behavioral Ecology|volume=15|issue=3|pages=438–445|doi=10.1093/beheco/arh027 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Dixson|first1=A.F.|year=1983|title=Observations on the evolution and behavioral significance of "sexual skin" in female primates|volume=13|pages=63–106|doi=10.1016/s0065-3454(08)60286-7|series=Advances in the Study of Behavior|isbn=9780120045136}}</ref> The color display on some female primates is called [[sexual swelling]]. This increase in redness has been shown to attract male counterparts, expressed by their increased activity in sex, self-stimulation, and attention towards the females. Reddening of the skin is also associated with sexual attraction in humans. When people experience romantic affection or sexual attraction towards another, [[blushing]] of the neck, face, and chest are common.<ref name=":03">{{Citation |last1=Pazda |first1=Adam D. |title=Color in romantic contexts in humans |date=2015 |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/handbook-of-color-psychology/color-in-romantic-contexts-in-humans/4339562314F7151D4DAC806AF3B0F663 |work=Handbook of Color Psychology |pages=531–545 |editor-last=Elliot |editor-first=Andrew J. |access-date=2023-03-13 |series=Cambridge Handbooks in Psychology |place=Cambridge |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-107-33793-0 |last2=Greitemeyer |first2=Tobias |editor2-last=Franklin |editor2-first=Anna |editor3-last=Fairchild |editor3-first=Mark D.}}</ref> Red skin can also indicate peak fertile stages of the [[menstrual cycle]], as estrogen levels increase in relation to progesterone levels and increase blood flow.<ref name=":03" /> Therefore, there are reasons to believe in the existence of [[evolution]]ary [[instinct]]s that associate red with [[fertility]], assuming the animal in question can perceive colour with its [[Eye#Colour perception|eyes]].
[[File:Wildlife_primate_monkey-of-japan_macaca-fuscata_closeup_31-05-2010.jpg|thumb|Near hairless red face [[Japanese macaque|Macaque]].]]
[[File:Mandrill_at_Singapore_Zoo.jpg|thumb|Colorful display on male baboons "Mandrill"]]

===Social conditioning===
The connection between the color red and sexual attraction may be a result of [[social conditioning]]. In ancient mythology<ref name=":03" />and folklore,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hutchings |first1=John |year=2004 |title=Color in folklore and tradition—The principles |journal=Color Research and Application |volume=29 |pages=57–66 |doi=10.1002/col.10212}}</ref> red is associated with fertility. Similarly, red was used as a symbol of fertility in the rituals of ancient civilizations.<ref name=":03" /> Women are thought to have worn the equivalent of a red [[lipstick]] as early as 10,000 B.C.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Low |first1=B |title=Evolutionary Biology and Human Social Behaviour: An Anthropological Perspective |publisher=Duxbury |year=1979 |isbn=978-0-87872-193-1 |editor1-last=Chagnon |editor1-first=Napoleon A. |pages=[https://archive.org/details/evolutionarybiol0000unse_x4e3/page/462 462–487] |chapter=Sexual selection and human ornamentation |editor2-last=Irons |editor2-first=William |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/evolutionarybiol0000unse_x4e3/page/462}}</ref> As such, the link between sexual receptiveness and red may be a result of [[social conditioning]]; however, this social conditioning may have originated for biological and evolutionary reasons, and is simply an extension of our primal instincts.

== Evidence for and against the red-attraction effect ==
A large portion of the research into the red-attraction effect focuses on the impact on the visual presentation of the color red paired with the presentation of a person to whom participants indicate their attraction. In a study by Pazda<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pazda |first1=Adam D. |last2=Elliot |first2=Andrew J. |last3=Greitemeyer |first3=Tobias |year=2011 |title=Sexy red: Perceived sexual receptivity mediates the red-attraction relation in men viewing woman |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |volume=48 |issue=3 |pages=787 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2011.12.009}}</ref> it was shown that females wearing red are rated more [[Sexual attraction|attractive]] by males. They explained this by referring to the biological aspect that sexually receptive women are more attractive because of them having a higher [[probability]] for engaging in [[Human sexual activity|sexual activity]], as well as a higher probability for the males to successfully [[Human reproduction|reproduce]]. Elliot and Nesta from the University of Rochester found that men reported more romantic feelings towards women when the women were wearing red or presented on a red background.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Red Enhances Men's Attraction To Women, Psychological Study Reveals |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2008/10/081028074323.htm |access-date=2023-04-24 |website=ScienceDaily |language=en}}</ref>

The same effect seems to work for females rating males. In a disputed<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Francis |first1=G |year=2013 |title=Publication bias in "Red, rank, and romance in women viewing men," by Elliot et al. (2010) |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |volume=142 |issue=1 |pages=292–6 |doi=10.1037/a0027923 |pmid=23398185}}</ref> study by Elliot,<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |last1=Elliot |first1=Andrew J. |last2=Niesta Kayser |first2=Daniela |last3=Greitemeyer |first3=Tobias |last4=Lichtenfeld |first4=Stephanie |last5=Gramzow |first5=Richard H. |last6=Maier |first6=Markus A. |last7=Liu |first7=Huijun |year=2010 |title=Red, Rank, and Romance in Women Viewing Men |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |volume=139 |issue=3 |pages=399–417 |citeseerx=10.1.1.335.828 |doi=10.1037/a0019689 |pmid=20677892}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Women attracted to men in red, research shows |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/08/100802101821.htm |access-date=2023-04-18 |website=ScienceDaily |language=en}}</ref> it was shown that males wearing red are rated more [[Sexual attraction|attractive]] by females. In another experiment of Elliot's study,<ref name=":2" /> women rated men as more attractive and reported being more sexually attracted to them when their photos were presented against a red background than when their photos were presented on a gray background. In an attempt to quell doubts about this study overall,<ref name=":1" /> other researchers have attempted to replicate this specific experiment. Overall, the multiple replication studies countered the conclusions of the original study, showing that changing the color of the background had no significant effect on the perceived attraction <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wagge |first1=Jordan R. |last2=Baciu |first2=Cristina |last3=Banas |first3=Kasia |last4=Nadler |first4=Joel T. |last5=Schwarz |first5=Sascha |last6=Weisberg |first6=Yanna |last7=IJzerman |first7=Hans |last8=Legate |first8=Nicole |last9=Grahe |first9=Jon |date=2019-01-01 |title=A Demonstration of the Collaborative Replication and Education Project: Replication Attempts of the Red-Romance Effect |journal=Collabra: Psychology |volume=5 |issue=1 |doi=10.1525/collabra.177 |s2cid=150104960 |issn=2474-7394|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Radoja |first=Dukata |title=Proceedings of the 3rd Human and Social Sciences at the Common Conference |chapter=The Role of Counselling in Medical Settings |date=2015-10-29 |volume=3 |chapter-url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/300249622 |pages=28–30 |doi=<!-- Virus center now 10.18638/hassacc.2015.3.1.169 --> |isbn=978-80-554-1124-8 }}</ref>

A large replication study by Peperkoorn et al. found no evidence for the red dress effect.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Peperkoorn |first1=L. S. |last2=Roberts |first2=S. C. |last3=Pollet |first3=T. V. |year=2016 |title=Revisiting the Red Effect on Attractiveness and Sexual Receptivity: No Effect of the Color Red on Human Mate Preferences |url=http://dspace.stir.ac.uk/bitstream/1893/24451/1/Peperkoorn_etal_EvolutionaryPsychology_2016.pdf |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |volume=14 |issue=October–December 2016 |pages=1–13 |doi=10.1177/1474704916673841 |pmid= |doi-access=free}}</ref> Indeed, another large experiment found no evidence that red color cues are systematically associated with attractiveness ratings, casting doubt on the underlying mechanisms typically used to explain the red-attraction effect.<ref name="williams20173">{{Cite journal |last1=Williams |first1=L. A. |last2=Schofield |first2=T. P. |last3=Whitford |first3=T. J. |year=2017 |title=Romantic Red: Testing the Characteristics of Color–Attraction Effects in a Novel Paradigm |journal=Collabra: Psychology |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=10 |doi=10.1525/collabra.54 |pmid= |doi-access=free}}</ref>

A study out of South Korea also supported the red-attraction link, as male participants rated a female model as more attractive when wearing a red shirt when compared to gray or blue shirts.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Jung |first1=Ilyung |last2=Kim |first2=Myung Shik |last3=Han |first3=Kwanghee |chapter=Red for Romance, Blue for Memory |date=2011 |editor-last=Stephanidis |editor-first=Constantine |title=HCI International 2011 – Posters' Extended Abstracts |series=Communications in Computer and Information Science |volume=173 |language=en |location=Berlin, Heidelberg |publisher=Springer |pages=284–288 |doi=10.1007/978-3-642-22098-2_57 |isbn=978-3-642-22098-2|s2cid=365542 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Some studies evaluated the red-romance link without investigating the role of red clothing. One study refuted the red-attraction link, showing that participants categorized red as being more related to anger-related words than to romance-related words, and that the romance-related words were categorized more with the color pink.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Winskel |first1=Heather |last2=Forrester |first2=Declan |last3=Hong |first3=Madelyn |last4=O'Connor |first4=Kourtney |date=2021-07-04 |title=Seeing red as anger or romance: an emotion categorisation task |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/20445911.2021.1936538 |journal=Journal of Cognitive Psychology |volume=33 |issue=5 |pages=581–594 |doi=10.1080/20445911.2021.1936538 |s2cid=236273286 |issn=2044-5911|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Another study examined the effect of the word "red" when verbally spoken, and found that when men were described as wearing red shirts they were rated as more attractive when compared to men described as wearing gray shirts, and when compared to men wearing green shirts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pazda |first1=Adam D. |last2=Elliot |first2=Andrew J. |date=2017-06-01 |title=Processing the Word Red can Enhance Women's Perceptions of Men's Attractiveness |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-016-9420-8 |journal=Current Psychology |language=en |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=316–323 |doi=10.1007/s12144-016-9420-8 |s2cid=255510631 |issn=1936-4733|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Therefore, this study also supported the red-attraction effect.

One study specifically evaluated the use of red clothing as a signal of sexual attraction, and found that women chose to wear a red shirt rather than a green shirt more often when they were told that they would be conversing with an attractive man than when they were told they were meeting with an unattractive man.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Elliot |first1=Andrew J. |last2=Greitemeyer |first2=Tobias |last3=Pazda |first3=Adam D. |date=2013-05-01 |title=Women's use of red clothing as a sexual signal in intersexual interaction |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002210311200203X |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=599–602 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2012.10.001 |issn=0022-1031|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Research conducted in Germany<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Berthold |first1=Anne |last2=Reese |first2=Gerhard |last3=Martin |first3=Judith |date=August 2017 |title=The effect of red color on perceived self-attractiveness: Red color and self-attractiveness |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ejsp.2238 |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology |language=en |volume=47 |issue=5 |pages=645–652 |doi=10.1002/ejsp.2238|url-access=subscription }}</ref> seems to extend the red-attraction effect to self-attraction. Participants wore either a red or blue shirt, and were then directed to rate their own attractiveness. Both males and females that wore red shirts rated themselves as more attractive, even when accounting for factors that typically influence self-attraction like BMI or mood.<ref name=":3" /> Red may be influential by increasing one's own perceived attractiveness, which in turn boosts self-confidence, and increased confidence is generally associated with higher levels of attraction from others.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Berthold |first=Anne |title=Want to impress on Valentine's Day? Then make sure to wear red |url=http://theconversation.com/want-to-impress-on-valentines-day-then-make-sure-to-wear-red-176816 |access-date=2023-04-24 |website=The Conversation |date=10 February 2022 |language=en}}</ref>

== Possible explanations ==
There seem to be multiple factors that influence the strength or presence of the red-attraction effect, which may account for the unclear evidence about the effect overall.

One possible explanation, that the red-attraction effect is ingrained and independent from societal influence, is supported by a study conducted in isolated rural communities of Burkina Faso.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Elliot |first1=Andrew J. |last2=Tracy |first2=Jessica L. |last3=Pazda |first3=Adam D. |last4=Beall |first4=Alec T. |date=2013-01-01 |title=Red enhances women's attractiveness to men: First evidence suggesting universality |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022103112001527 |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=49 |issue=1 |pages=165–168 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2012.07.017 |issn=0022-1031|url-access=subscription }}</ref> When males were asked to indicate the attraction of women photographed against red and blue backgrounds, those in the red condition rated the women as more attractive.<ref name=":4" /> Therefore, this study supports the universality of the red-attraction effect. However, they did not report a stronger sexual desire towards the women, which led to the conclusion that the red-attraction effect may be constrained by culturally appropriate expressions of attraction.<ref name=":4" /> This may explain why some studies have not supported the red-attraction effect.

Another study<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Wen |first1=Fangfang |last2=Zuo |first2=Bin |last3=Wu |first3=Yang |last4=Sun |first4=Shan |last5=Liu |first5=Ke |date=2014-10-01 |title=Red is Romantic, but Only for Feminine Females: Sexual Dimorphism Moderates Red Effect on Sexual Attraction |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |language=en |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=719–735 |doi=10.1177/147470491401200404 |pmid=25300050 |s2cid=11162521 |issn=1474-7049|doi-access=free |pmc=10480888 }}</ref> examined the role of [[sexual dimorphism]] as a possible moderator. Sexual dimorphism, morphological differences between the sexes of a species, are the differences between masculine and feminine facial features in this context. The study, conducted in China, found that the red-attraction effect only held true when males were rating faces with highly feminine cues.<ref name=":5" /> Feminine cues were determined by averaging several female faces together, and averaging several male faces together and isolating the differences. When males rated faces with more masculinity cues, the color red seemed to have no impact on perceived attraction.<ref name=":5" /> Sexual dimorphism could be a possible explanation for variying results in studies of the red-attraction effect.

However, there may also be reason to conclude that the red-romance effect could be due to the awareness of the proposed connection between the color red and the object or person that is being rated. One study concludes that the typical red-attraction link only occurs when an explicit pairing of the color red and the target occurs, rather than an implicit or unconscious cue.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |title=Romantic Red: Testing the Characteristics of Color–Attraction Effects in a Novel Paradigm |url=https://online.ucpress.edu/collabra/article/3/1/10/112367/Romantic-Red-Testing-the-Characteristics-of-Color |access-date=2023-03-14 |journal=Collabra: Psychology |year=2017 |doi=10.1525/collabra.54 |last1=Williams |first1=Lisa A. |last2=Schofield |first2=Timothy P. |last3=Whitford |first3=Thomas J. |volume=3 |doi-access=free }}</ref> As such, high awareness of the color-target pair is a necessary constraint of the red-attraction effect.<ref name=":6" />

The red-attraction effect is strongly limited by a baseline or prior level of attraction. The red-attraction effect only held true when males rated females that they initially rated as attractive.<ref name="Young 146–151">{{Cite journal |last=Young |first=Steven G. |date=March 2015 |title=The effect of red on male perceptions of female attractiveness: Moderation by baseline attractiveness of female faces: Red and attractiveness |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ejsp.2098 |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology |language=en |volume=45 |issue=2 |pages=146–151 |doi=10.1002/ejsp.2098|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In females rated as unattractive, the presentation of the color red did not increase perceived attraction.<ref name="Young 146–151"/>

== Conclusion ==
Further research on the red-attraction effect could improve on the methods used to evaluate this effect, as the quality of the current studies on the red-attraction effect is not favorable.<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last1=Lehmann |first1=Gabrielle K. |last2=Elliot |first2=Andrew J. |last3=Calin-Jageman |first3=Robert J. |date=2018-10-01 |title=Meta-Analysis of the Effect of Red on Perceived Attractiveness |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |language=en |volume=16 |issue=4 |pages=147470491880241 |doi=10.1177/1474704918802412 |pmid=30282470 |s2cid=52914436 |issn=1474-7049|doi-access=free |pmc=10480976 }}</ref> The majority of existing research fails to consider color production and color presentation,<ref name=":7" /> and future research could also take into account sample size and the complexity of [[color psychology]]. Additionally, the conclusions for male participants are clearer than those for female participants.<ref name=":7" /> Studies of higher quality could yield a clearer conclusion about the true effect, if any, of red on attraction.

* [[Peafowl]]
* [[Ribbon-tailed astrapia]]
